schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning the silver trumpets: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses, now to give the law of the two silver trumpets, for calling the assembly and the journeying of the camps (ch. 10, the speaking, God's command, the new section, the occasion, after the camp was ordered and the Levites consecrated, now the means of signalling the assembly and the march, the trumpets by which God would direct the movements of his people, God giving the law of the trumpets, showing his care to order the gathering and the going of his people, the signals of his will for the camp); the LORD speaking to Moses (the law of the trumpets) — the new section (the silver trumpets); the LORD spake unto Moses — the law of the trumpets introduced (the providence, God ordering the signals of the camp) — Henry, Gill, JFB.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying
the LORD speaking concerning the silver trumpets
God giving the law of the trumpets, the signals for the assembly and the march (ch. 10)

schools.summary: The two silver trumpets for the assembly and the march: 'make thee two trumpets of silver; of a whole piece shalt thou make them: that thou mayest use them for the calling of the assembly, and for the journeying of the camps' — the trumpets — 'two trumpets of silver... for the calling of the assembly, and for the journeying of the camps': two trumpets of beaten silver, of one whole piece, made to call the people together and to signal the march (the witnesses noting the significance — the two uses, the calling of the assembly, the gathering of the people to God, and the journeying of the camps, the signal to set forward, the lesson — the trumpets a figure of the voice and call of God to his people, the silver of redemption, the trumpet the call that gathers and that moves, the ministry of the word, the gospel trumpet that calls the people of God, 'lift up thy voice like a trumpet,' Isaiah 58:1, the certain sound of the trumpet that calls to the battle and the assembly, 1 Corinthians 14:8, the figure — the call of God by his word and Spirit, gathering his people to himself and directing their way, and supremely the last trumpet that shall gather the saints, 'the trumpet shall sound, and the dead shall be raised,' 1 Corinthians 15:52, 'the trump of God... the dead in Christ shall rise first,' 1 Thessalonians 4:16, the trumpet of God that calls his people to assemble, to march, to worship, and at last to glory); the two silver trumpets of one whole piece (the beaten silver, the instruments of signalling) — the trumpets (the silver instruments); the calling of the assembly and the journeying of the camps (the gathering and the march, the call of God, Isaiah 58:1) — the figure (the gospel trumpet, the last trump, 1 Corinthians 15:52, the providence, the trumpets used to gather and to move) — Calvin, Keil, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What do the silver trumpets signify?

sides.position: The call of God by his word and Spirit, gathering his people and directing their way (Isaiah 58:1; 1 Corinthians 14:8) - a figure of the gospel trumpet and supremely of the last trumpet that shall gather and raise the saints (1 Corinthians 15:52; 1 Thessalonians 4:16)

themes: Make thee two trumpets of silver; of a whole piece shalt thou make them: that thou mayest use them for the calling of the assembly, and for the journeying of the camps
the two silver trumpets for the assembly and the march (the crux of the call of God to his people)
the call of God by his word and Spirit, gathering his people and directing their way (Isaiah 58:1)
a figure of the last trumpet that shall gather the saints (1 Corinthians 15:52; 1 Thessalonians 4:16)

schools.summary: The blowing of both trumpets to gather all the assembly: 'and when they shall blow with them, all the assembly shall assemble themselves to thee at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation' — when both trumpets were blown, the whole assembly gathered to Moses at the tabernacle door (the when they shall blow with them, both trumpets sounded, the all the assembly shall assemble themselves, the whole congregation gathered, the at the door of the tabernacle, the place of gathering, before God, the figure, the call that gathers the whole people to God, the trumpet of both blown summoning all, the figure of the gospel call that gathers the people to the Lord, 'gather my saints together unto me,' Psalm 50:5, the assembly gathered to God at his house, the whole people called together); both trumpets blown (the full call) — the call (both sounded); 'all the assembly shall assemble themselves... at the door of the tabernacle' (the whole congregation gathered to God, Psalm 50:5, the figure, the call that gathers the people to the Lord, the providence, the assembly gathered, the princes' call to follow, vv.4) — Gill.

the blowing of both trumpets to gather all the assembly
the call that gathers the whole people to God at his house

schools.summary: The one trumpet for the gathering of the princes: 'and if they blow but with one trumpet, then the princes, which are heads of the thousands of Israel, shall gather themselves unto thee' — when only one trumpet was blown, the princes alone, the heads of the thousands, gathered to Moses (the if they blow but with one trumpet, the single blast, the then the princes... shall gather themselves, the leaders called, the heads of the thousands of Israel, the chief men, the figure, the distinct signals for the whole and for the leaders, the orderly summoning, each call understood, the wisdom of the ordered signals, the people knowing by the sound whom God called, the figure of the different calls of God, to the whole and to the rulers, each in his place); the one trumpet (the signal for the princes) — the signal (the single blast); 'the princes... shall gather themselves unto thee' (the leaders called by the single trumpet, the heads of the thousands, the figure, the ordered signals, each call understood, the providence, the princes gathered, the alarm to follow, vv.5-6) — Ellicott, Gill.

the distinct signals for the whole and for the leaders, each call understood
the orderly summoning of God's people

schools.summary: The alarm for the eastern camps to set forward: 'when ye blow an alarm, then the camps that lie on the east parts shall go forward' — a different blast, an alarm (a broken, urging sound), signalled the camps on the east to set forward first (the when ye blow an alarm, the alarm, the urging blast, distinct from the assembly call, the the camps that lie on the east parts shall go forward, Judah's camp on the east, first to march, Numbers 2:3, the figure, the alarm that moves the camp, the distinct signal for the march, the order of the march, the east first, each camp in its turn at the trumpet's sound, the people moving at God's signal, going forward when he calls); the alarm (the urging blast for the march) — the signal (the alarm); 'the camps that lie on the east parts shall go forward' (the eastern camps, Judah, first to march, Numbers 2:3, the figure, the order of the march at the signal, the providence, the east first, the south to follow, vv.6) — Clarke, Gill.

schools.summary: The second alarm for the southern camps: 'when ye blow an alarm the second time, then the camps that lie on the south side shall take their journey: they shall blow an alarm for their journeys' — the second alarm signalled the camps on the south to march, the alarm being the signal for the journeys (the the second time, the second alarm, the the camps that lie on the south side shall take their journey, Reuben's camp on the south, second to march, Numbers 2:10, the they shall blow an alarm for their journeys, the alarm the march signal, the figure, the ordered march, each camp in its turn at the alarm, the distinct signals that moved the camps in their appointed order, the people going forward in order, none before his time, each at God's signal); the second alarm for the southern camps (Reuben, Numbers 2:10) — the signal (the south second); 'they shall blow an alarm for their journeys' (the alarm the signal for the march, each journey signalled, the figure, the ordered march at the signal, the providence, the march by the alarm, the assembly call distinguished, vv.7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The difference between the assembly call and the alarm: 'but when the congregation is to be gathered together, ye shall blow, but ye shall not sound an alarm' — to gather the assembly, the trumpets blown with a steady sound, not the broken alarm of the march (the ye shall blow, the steady blast for the assembly, the but ye shall not sound an alarm, not the urging, broken blast of the march, the figure, the distinct sounds for distinct ends, the steady call to gather and the alarm to move, the wisdom of the clear signals, each understood, 'if the trumpet give an uncertain sound, who shall prepare himself to the battle?' 1 Corinthians 14:8, the certain sound of God's call, the clear word that gathers and that directs); the steady blast for the assembly (the gathering call) — the call (the steady blast); 'but ye shall not sound an alarm' (not the broken alarm of the march, the distinct sounds, 1 Corinthians 14:8, the figure, the certain sound of God's call, the providence, the clear signals, the priests to blow, vv.8) — JFB, Gill.

schools.summary: The priests as the blowers of the trumpets: 'and the sons of Aaron, the priests, shall blow with the trumpets; and they shall be to you for an ordinance for ever throughout your generations' — the priests, the sons of Aaron, to blow the trumpets, an ordinance forever (the the sons of Aaron, the priests, shall blow, the priests the trumpeters, not the Levites or the people, the an ordinance for ever throughout your generations, the lasting law, the figure, the priests appointed to sound the call of God, the ministry of the word committed to those God sets apart, the figure of the ministry that sounds the gospel trumpet, the appointed heralds of God's call, 'how shall they hear without a preacher?' Romans 10:14-15, the trumpet of God sounded by his appointed servants, the lasting ordinance of the call); the priests the trumpeters (the appointed blowers, Romans 10:14-15) — the priests (the heralds of the call); 'they shall be to you for an ordinance for ever' (the lasting law, the trumpets sounded by the priests through the generations, the figure, the ministry that sounds God's call, Romans 10:14-15, the providence, the priests the blowers, the alarm in war to follow, vv.9) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The alarm in war and the remembrance before the LORD: 'and if ye go to war in your land against the enemy that oppresseth you, then ye shall blow an alarm with the trumpets; and ye shall be remembered before the LORD your God, and ye shall be saved from your enemies' — the alarm and the remembrance — 'ye shall blow an alarm with the trumpets; and ye shall be remembered before the LORD your God, and ye shall be saved': when Israel went to war against the oppressing enemy, the alarm with the trumpets an appeal to God, by which they would be remembered before him and saved (the witnesses noting the significance — the alarm in war an appeal to God, the trumpet not a mere military signal but a cry to the LORD, a calling upon him in the day of battle, the lesson — that God remembers his people when they cry to him in distress, and saves them, the trumpet a prayer, the appeal to God in the day of trouble, 'call upon me in the day of trouble: I will deliver thee,' Psalm 50:15, 'in their affliction they will seek me early,' Hosea 5:15, the ye shall be remembered before the LORD, God's gracious remembrance of his people's cry, the assurance of his help, the figure — the believer's appeal to God in the spiritual warfare, the cry that God hears and answers, 'the LORD is my rock... I will call upon the LORD, who is worthy to be praised: so shall I be saved from mine enemies,' 2 Samuel 22:4; Psalm 18:3, the truth that the people who cry to God in their battles are remembered and delivered, the prayer that brings God's help, the trumpet of faith that calls upon the LORD); the alarm blown in war (the appeal to God, the cry of the battle) — the alarm (the cry to the LORD); the people remembered before God and saved (the cry heard, the deliverance, Psalm 50:15) — the figure (the appeal to God in distress, Psalm 18:3, the providence, the people remembered and saved, the trumpet of the feasts to follow, vv.10) — Calvin, Keil, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the alarm in war signify?

sides.position: The trumpet a cry to the LORD in the day of battle, by which his people are remembered before him and delivered (Psalm 50:15; Hosea 5:15) - a figure of the believer's appeal to God in the spiritual warfare, the prayer that God hears and answers (2 Samuel 22:4; Psalm 18:3)

schools.summary: The trumpets over the offerings as a memorial before God: 'also in the day of your gladness, and in your solemn days, and in the beginnings of your months, ye shall blow with the trumpets over your burnt offerings, and over the sacrifices of your peace offerings; that they may be to you for a memorial before your God: I am the LORD your God' — the trumpets also blown in the joyful days, the feasts, and the new moons, over the offerings, as a memorial before God (the the day of your gladness... solemn days... beginnings of your months, the feasts and new moons, the joyful seasons, the blow with the trumpets over your burnt offerings, and over the sacrifices of your peace offerings, the trumpets sounded over the offerings, the for a memorial before your God, the offerings and the trumpets a memorial, bringing the people to God's gracious remembrance, the figure, the trumpets of joy and worship, the praise that ascends with the offerings, the figure of the praise and prayer that rise to God in worship, the joyful sound of the people of God, 'make a joyful noise unto the LORD... with trumpets and sound of cornet,' Psalm 98:4-6, 'blow up the trumpet in the new moon, in the time appointed, on our solemn feast day,' Psalm 81:3, the worship that God remembers, the trumpet of praise before him); the trumpets over the offerings in the feasts and new moons (the joyful worship, Psalm 81:3) — the feasts (the trumpets of joy); 'that they may be to you for a memorial before your God' (the offerings and trumpets a memorial before God, the worship remembered, Psalm 98:6, the figure, the praise that rises to God, the providence, the trumpets of worship, the setting forth from Sinai to follow, vv.11) — Calvin, Gill.

schools.summary: The cloud taken up, signalling the departure from Sinai: 'and it came to pass on the twentieth day of the second month, in the second year, that the cloud was taken up from off the tabernacle of the testimony' — at the appointed time, the cloud lifted from the tabernacle, the signal to set forward from Sinai (the the twentieth day of the second month, in the second year, the precise date, nearly a year after Sinai was reached, the the cloud was taken up from off the tabernacle, the signal to march, the cloud the guide, the figure, the cloud the visible token of God's presence and guidance, taken up to lead them on, the departure from Sinai, the journey to Canaan begun, the truth that God's people move at his signal, the cloud taken up the call to go forward, 'the LORD went before them... in a pillar of a cloud, to lead them the way,' Exodus 13:21, the people following the guidance of God, going when he goes before); the precise date of the departure (nearly a year at Sinai) — the date (the time of setting forth); 'the cloud was taken up from off the tabernacle' (the cloud the signal and guide, the departure from Sinai, Exodus 13:21, the figure, God's people moving at his guidance, the providence, the cloud taken up, the journey begun, vv.12-13) — Henry, Gill, Calvin.

schools.summary: The journey out of Sinai and the cloud resting in Paran: 'and the children of Israel took their journeys out of the wilderness of Sinai; and the cloud rested in the wilderness of Paran' — Israel journeyed from the wilderness of Sinai, following the cloud, which rested in the wilderness of Paran (the took their journeys out of the wilderness of Sinai, the departure from Sinai, the journey begun, the the cloud rested in the wilderness of Paran, the cloud leading them to Paran, the next resting place, the figure, the people following the cloud, journeying and resting at God's direction, the pilgrimage of God's people guided by his presence, going and stopping as the cloud led, the truth that the way of God's people is ordered by him, the journeys and the rests appointed by the cloud, the figure of the pilgrim life led by God, Exodus 40:36-37); the departure from Sinai (the journey begun) — the journey (out of Sinai); 'the cloud rested in the wilderness of Paran' (the cloud leading them to Paran, the next rest, Exodus 40:36-37, the figure, the people guided by the cloud, journeying and resting, the providence, the journey from Sinai, the order of the march to follow, vv.13-28) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The first march by the commandment of the LORD: 'and they first took their journey according to the commandment of the LORD by the hand of Moses' — this first journey from Sinai made by the LORD's commandment, through Moses (the they first took their journey, the first march, the departure begun, the according to the commandment of the LORD by the hand of Moses, at God's word, through his mediator, the figure, the journey ordered by God, not by the people's will but at his command, the truth that the movements of God's people are directed by his word, the march begun at his bidding, the figure of the obedient pilgrimage, going at God's command, 'at the commandment of the LORD they journeyed,' Numbers 9:23, the people moving as God directs, the way ordered by his word through his servant); the first journey from Sinai (the departure) — the journey (the first march); 'according to the commandment of the LORD by the hand of Moses' (at God's word through Moses, the march commanded, Numbers 9:23, the figure, the journey ordered by God, the providence, the march by command, the order of the tribes to follow, vv.14-28) — JFB, Gill.

schools.summary: The Kohathites bearing the sanctuary and the tabernacle set up before them: 'and the Kohathites set forward, bearing the sanctuary: and the other did set up the tabernacle against they came' — the Kohathites set forward bearing the holy things (the sanctuary), and the Gershonites and Merarites, who went before, set up the tabernacle before the Kohathites arrived (the the Kohathites set forward, bearing the sanctuary, the Kohathites bearing the ark and the holy furniture, Numbers 4:4-15, the the other did set up the tabernacle against they came, the framework-bearers setting up the tent before the holy things came, the figure, the holy things borne after the framework, that the dwelling be ready to receive them, the careful order that the sanctuary might be set up before the ark and vessels arrived, the figure of the order of God's house, every part in its time, the holy things honored by the prepared dwelling, the reverence in the bearing of the sanctuary); the Kohathites bearing the holy things (the ark and furniture, Numbers 4:4-15) — the bearing (the sanctuary borne); 'the other did set up the tabernacle against they came' (the framework-bearers setting up the tent before the holy things came, the order, the figure, the dwelling ready to receive the holy things, the providence, the order of the bearing, Ephraim's camp to follow, vv.22) — Wesley, Gill.

themes: And the Kohathites set forward, bearing the sanctuary: and the other did set up the tabernacle against they came
the Kohathites bearing the sanctuary and the tabernacle set up before them

the careful order and reverence in the bearing of the sanctuary

schools.summary: The standard of Ephraim setting forward in its order: 'and the standard of the camp of the children of Ephraim set forward according to their armies: and over his host was Elishama the son of Ammihud' — Ephraim's camp, with its standard, set forward in its place, Elishama leading its host (the the standard of the camp of Ephraim set forward, Ephraim's camp, third in the march, Numbers 2:18, the over his host was Elishama the son of Ammihud, the prince of Ephraim, the figure, Ephraim's camp in its order, after the sanctuary, each camp in its appointed place, the standards set forward in turn, the orderly march of God's people, every camp in its station, the figure of the church in array, each following his standard in order); Ephraim's standard in its place (the third camp, Numbers 2:18) — the order (Ephraim's place); 'over his host was Elishama the son of Ammihud' (the prince of Ephraim, the appointed head, the figure, each camp in its order, the providence, Ephraim's camp, Manasseh and Benjamin to follow, vv.23-24) — Gill.

themes: And the standard of the camp of the children of Ephraim set forward according to their armies: and over his host was Elishama the son of Ammihud
the standard of Ephraim setting forward in its order
Ephraim's camp in its place after the sanctuary, each camp in its order (Numbers 2:18)
the orderly march of God's people

schools.summary: The prince over the host of Manasseh in the march: 'and over the host of the tribe of the children of Manasseh was Gamaliel the son of Pedahzur' — in the ordered march, Gamaliel the son of Pedahzur led the host of Manasseh (the over the host of Manasseh, the tribe in its place in the line of march, the Gamaliel the son of Pedahzur, the named prince leading his tribe, Numbers 2, the figure, each tribe in its appointed place in the march, each led by its prince, the order of God's people on the journey, every tribe and every leader in his station, the figure of the church where each has his place and his order, the host marching in array under their appointed heads, the orderly pilgrimage, none out of place, each following his standard, 1 Corinthians 14:40); Manasseh in its place in the march (the ordered line, Numbers 2) — the order (the tribe's place); 'Gamaliel the son of Pedahzur' (the named prince leading his tribe, the appointed head, the figure, each tribe led by its prince, the order of the march, 1 Corinthians 14:40, the providence, the tribe in its place, the next in the line to follow) — Gill.

themes: And over the host of the tribe of the children of Manasseh was Gamaliel the son of Pedahzur
the prince over the host of Manasseh in the march
each tribe in its appointed place, led by its prince (Numbers 2)
the orderly pilgrimage of God's people in array under their heads (1 Corinthians 14:40)

schools.summary: The prince over the host of Benjamin in the march: 'and over the host of the tribe of the children of Benjamin was Abidan the son of Gideoni' — in the ordered march, Abidan the son of Gideoni led the host of Benjamin (the over the host of Benjamin, the tribe in its place in the line of march, the Abidan the son of Gideoni, the named prince leading his tribe, Numbers 2, the figure, each tribe in its appointed place in the march, each led by its prince, the order of God's people on the journey, every tribe and every leader in his station, the figure of the church where each has his place and his order, the host marching in array under their appointed heads, the orderly pilgrimage, none out of place, each following his standard, 1 Corinthians 14:40); Benjamin in its place in the march (the ordered line, Numbers 2) — the order (the tribe's place); 'Abidan the son of Gideoni' (the named prince leading his tribe, the appointed head, the figure, each tribe led by its prince, the order of the march, 1 Corinthians 14:40, the providence, the tribe in its place, the next in the line to follow) — Gill.

themes: And over the host of the tribe of the children of Benjamin was Abidan the son of Gideoni
the prince over the host of Benjamin in the march
each tribe in its appointed place, led by its prince (Numbers 2)
the orderly pilgrimage of God's people in array under their heads (1 Corinthians 14:40)

schools.summary: The standard of Dan as the rearward of all the camps: 'and the standard of the camp of the children of Dan set forward, which was the rereward of all the camps throughout their hosts: and over his host was Ahiezer the son of Ammishaddai' — Dan's camp, with its standard, set forward last, the rearward (rear guard) of all the camps, Ahiezer leading its host (the the standard of the camp of Dan set forward, Dan's camp, last in the march, Numbers 2:25, the which was the rereward of all the camps, the rear guard, gathering up the host, the over his host was Ahiezer the son of Ammishaddai, the prince of Dan, the figure, Dan the rear guard, closing and guarding the march, the order that set the strong tribe at the rear, to gather the weak and protect the host, the figure of the care for all the people, none left behind, the rear guard bringing up the company, the truth that God orders the whole march, front and rear, 'the God of Israel will be your rereward,' Isaiah 52:12); Dan the rear guard (the last camp, gathering the host, Numbers 2:25) — the order (Dan the rearward); 'over his host was Ahiezer the son of Ammishaddai' (the prince of Dan, the appointed head, the figure, the rear guard, the care for all, Isaiah 52:12, the providence, Dan the rearward, Asher and Naphtali to follow, vv.26-27) — Gill.

themes: And the standard of the camp of the children of Dan set forward, which was the rereward of all the camps throughout their hosts: and over his host was Ahiezer the son of Ammishaddai
the standard of Dan as the rearward of all the camps
Dan the rear guard, gathering and protecting the host, none left behind (Numbers 2:25)
a figure that God orders the whole march, the God of Israel the rearward of his people (Isaiah 52:12)

schools.summary: The prince over the host of Asher in the march: 'and over the host of the tribe of the children of Asher was Pagiel the son of Ocran' — in the ordered march, Pagiel the son of Ocran led the host of Asher (the over the host of Asher, the tribe in its place in the line of march, the Pagiel the son of Ocran, the named prince leading his tribe, Numbers 2, the figure, each tribe in its appointed place in the march, each led by its prince, the order of God's people on the journey, every tribe and every leader in his station, the figure of the church where each has his place and his order, the host marching in array under their appointed heads, the orderly pilgrimage, none out of place, each following his standard, 1 Corinthians 14:40); Asher in its place in the march (the ordered line, Numbers 2) — the order (the tribe's place); 'Pagiel the son of Ocran' (the named prince leading his tribe, the appointed head, the figure, each tribe led by its prince, the order of the march, 1 Corinthians 14:40, the providence, the tribe in its place, the next in the line to follow) — Gill.

themes: And over the host of the tribe of the children of Asher was Pagiel the son of Ocran

each tribe in its appointed place, led by its prince (Numbers 2)

schools.summary: The prince over the host of Naphtali in the march: 'and over the host of the tribe of the children of Naphtali was Ahira the son of Enan' — in the ordered march, Ahira the son of Enan led the host of Naphtali (the over the host of Naphtali, the tribe in its place in the line of march, the Ahira the son of Enan, the named prince leading his tribe, Numbers 2, the figure, each tribe in its appointed place in the march, each led by its prince, the order of God's people on the journey, every tribe and every leader in his station, the figure of the church where each has his place and his order, the host marching in array under their appointed heads, the orderly pilgrimage, none out of place, each following his standard, 1 Corinthians 14:40); Naphtali in its place in the march (the ordered line, Numbers 2) — the order (the tribe's place); 'Ahira the son of Enan' (the named prince leading his tribe, the appointed head, the figure, each tribe led by its prince, the order of the march, 1 Corinthians 14:40, the providence, the tribe in its place, the next in the line to follow) — Gill.

themes: And over the host of the tribe of the children of Naphtali was Ahira the son of Enan

schools.summary: The summary of the ordered journeyings of Israel: 'thus were the journeyings of the children of Israel according to their armies, when they set forward' — this summing up the order of the march: the tribes journeyed in their appointed armies and stations (the thus were the journeyings of the children of Israel, the ordered march summed, the according to their armies, each tribe in its host and place, the when they set forward, the order kept whenever they marched, the figure, the orderly march of God's people, every tribe in its station, the whole host moving in array, the figure of the order of God's people, each in his place, the church marching in array under God's direction, the beauty of order in God's house and host, 'let all things be done decently and in order,' 1 Corinthians 14:40, the people of God a well-ordered army on the pilgrimage); the ordered journeyings summed (each tribe in its place) — the order (the march summed); 'when they set forward' (the order kept whenever they marched, the army in array, 1 Corinthians 14:40, the figure, the church in order on the pilgrimage, the providence, the ordered march, Hobab and the journey to follow, vv.29-36) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Thus were the journeyings of the children of Israel according to their armies, when they set forward
the summary of the ordered journeyings of Israel
every tribe in its station, the whole host moving in array
the people of God a well-ordered army on the pilgrimage (1 Corinthians 14:40)

schools.summary: Moses's invitation to Hobab to share Israel's blessing: 'and Moses said unto Hobab... come thou with us, and we will do thee good: for the LORD hath spoken good concerning Israel' — the offer — 'come thou with us, and we will do thee good: for the LORD hath spoken good concerning Israel': Moses inviting Hobab, his kinsman by marriage, to journey with Israel to the promised land and share the good God had promised (the witnesses noting the significance — the invitation to come with God's people and share their blessing, the offer of the good that God had promised Israel, extended to Hobab the Midianite, the stranger brought in to share the inheritance, the lesson — the gospel invitation to come with the people of God, the call to the outsider to join the pilgrim company and share the promised good, the welcome of the stranger into the blessing of the covenant, 'we are journeying unto the place of which the LORD said, I will give it you' — the people of God on pilgrimage to the promised rest, inviting others to come along, the ground — 'for the LORD hath spoken good concerning Israel,' the invitation rests on God's promise, the good assured to his people, the certainty of the blessing that draws others in, the figure — the gospel call to come with the church to the heavenly Canaan, the believer's invitation to the stranger, 'we have found him, of whom Moses... did write... come and see,' John 1:45-46, 'let us go up to the house of the LORD,' Isaiah 2:3, the welcome of all who will to share the promised good, the figure of the kindness that would do good to the stranger and bring him into the blessing of God's people, the figure of the open invitation to the pilgrimage and the promised rest); the people journeying to the promised land (the pilgrimage to the inheritance) — the pilgrimage (the journey to the promised rest); the invitation to share the good God had promised (the stranger welcomed to the blessing, John 1:46) — the figure (the gospel call to come with God's people, Isaiah 2:3, the providence, Hobab invited, the second appeal to follow, vv.30-31) — Calvin, Ellicott, Henry, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does Moses's invitation to Hobab signify?

sides.position: The invitation to come with God's pilgrim people to the promised land and share the blessing, resting on God's promise of good to his people - a figure of the gospel call to come with the church to the heavenly Canaan and share the promised rest (John 1:45-46; Isaiah 2:3), the welcome of the stranger into the blessing of the covenant

themes: And Moses said unto Hobab... come thou with us, and we will do thee good: for the LORD hath spoken good concerning Israel
Moses's invitation to Hobab to share Israel's blessing (the crux of the gospel invitation to come with the people of God)
the invitation to come with God's pilgrim people and share the promised good, resting on God's promise
a figure of the gospel call to the heavenly Canaan and the welcome of the stranger (John 1:45-46; Isaiah 2:3)

schools.summary: Hobab's reluctance to go: 'and he said unto him, I will not go; but I will depart to mine own land, and to my kindred' — Hobab at first declined, choosing to return to his own land and kindred (the I will not go, the refusal, the first answer, the but I will depart to mine own land, and to my kindred, the pull of home and kin, the figure, the reluctance to leave the old for the promised good, the drawing back to the familiar, the figure of the heart that hesitates to follow God's people, held by the love of home and kindred, the choice between the old land and the pilgrimage, the temptation to turn back, 'no man, having put his hand to the plough, and looking back, is fit for the kingdom of God,' Luke 9:62, the reluctance that the second appeal would seek to overcome, vv.31); Hobab's first refusal (the drawing back) — the refusal (the first answer); 'I will depart to mine own land, and to my kindred' (the pull of home and kin, the love of the familiar, Luke 9:62, the figure, the reluctance to follow, the providence, the refusal, the second appeal to follow, vv.31) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And he said unto him, I will not go; but I will depart to mine own land, and to my kindred
Hobab's reluctance to go
the reluctance to leave the old for the promised good, the pull of home and kin
the heart that hesitates to follow God's people (Luke 9:62)

schools.summary: Moses's second appeal, Hobab as eyes for the camp: 'and he said, Leave us not, I pray thee; forasmuch as thou knowest how we are to encamp in the wilderness, and thou mayest be to us instead of eyes' — Moses urged Hobab again, not to leave, for his knowledge of the wilderness would be as eyes to guide the camp (the Leave us not, I pray thee, the earnest second appeal, the thou knowest how we are to encamp in the wilderness, Hobab's knowledge of the desert ways, the thou mayest be to us instead of eyes, his guidance valued, the use of his knowledge, the figure, the use of human means and knowledge even where God guides by the cloud, the wisdom that values the help of the experienced, some noting the apparent tension — Israel led by the cloud, yet Moses sought Hobab's eyes — and reading it as God's ordinary use of means alongside his guidance, not distrust of the cloud but the lawful use of knowledge, the figure of the value of every gift in the service of God's people, the experienced welcomed for the good of the company, the earnest desire to keep the stranger among the people of God); Moses's earnest second appeal (Hobab's knowledge valued) — the appeal (the urging not to leave); 'thou mayest be to us instead of eyes' (Hobab's guidance as eyes, the use of means alongside the cloud, the figure, the lawful use of human help in God's service, the providence, Hobab pressed, the journey continuing, vv.32-33) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And he said, Leave us not, I pray thee; forasmuch as thou knowest how we are to encamp in the wilderness, and thou mayest be to us instead of eyes
Moses's second appeal, Hobab as eyes for the camp
the value of human knowledge in the service of God's people, the use of means alongside God's guidance
the experienced welcomed for the good of the company

schools.summary: The promise to share with Hobab the good God would do: 'and it shall be, if thou go with us, yea, it shall be, that what goodness the LORD shall do unto us, the same will we do unto thee' — Moses promising that whatever good God did to Israel, they would share with Hobab (the if thou go with us, the condition, joining the pilgrimage, the what goodness the LORD shall do unto us, the same will we do unto thee, the promise to share the blessing, the figure, the promise that the stranger who joins God's people shares their blessing, the truth that those who cast in their lot with the people of God partake of the good God does them, the figure of the gospel promise, that whoever comes with the church shares the inheritance, 'partakers of the promise in Christ by the gospel,' Ephesians 3:6, the welcome of the stranger into the full fellowship of the blessing, the good of God's people shared with all who join them); the condition of joining the pilgrimage (casting in his lot) — the condition (joining God's people); 'what goodness the LORD shall do unto us, the same will we do unto thee' (the promise to share the good, the stranger partaking, Ephesians 3:6, the figure, the gospel promise of shared inheritance, the providence, the promise made, the journey to the rest to follow, vv.33) — Gill.

themes: And it shall be, if thou go with us, yea, it shall be, that what goodness the LORD shall do unto us, the same will we do unto thee
the promise to share with Hobab the good God would do
the stranger who joins God's people sharing their blessing
the gospel promise that whoever comes with the church shares the inheritance (Ephesians 3:6)

schools.summary: The ark of the covenant going before to find a resting place: 'and they departed from the mount of the LORD three days' journey: and the ark of the covenant of the LORD went before them in the three days' journey, to search out a resting place for them' — the ark going before — 'the ark of the covenant of the LORD went before them... to search out a resting place for them': as they departed from Sinai, the ark of the covenant went before the host to seek out a resting place for them (the witnesses noting the significance — the ark, the throne of God's presence, leading the way and seeking the rest for his people, the symbol of God himself going before to find them a place of rest, the lesson — that God leads his people and goes before them to prepare their rest, the ark, the place of the mercy seat and the presence, going ahead to seek the resting place, God himself their guide and the seeker of their rest, the figure — Christ going before his people, the forerunner, to prepare a place and a rest for them, 'whither the forerunner is for us entered, even Jesus,' Hebrews 6:20, 'I go to prepare a place for you,' John 14:2-3, the ark of the covenant a figure of Christ in whom God dwells, going before to find the rest, leading his people to the promised rest, 'there remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God,' Hebrews 4:9, the three days' journey, the ark seeking the rest, a figure of the Lord who leads his pilgrim people and prepares their everlasting rest, the truth that God's presence both leads the way and secures the rest, the people following the ark to their resting place, the figure of Christ the forerunner leading to the promised rest); the three days' journey from Sinai (the departure from the mount of God) — the journey (the departure from Sinai); the ark going before to seek the resting place (God leading and preparing the rest, Hebrews 6:20) — the figure (Christ the forerunner, John 14:2-3, the providence, the ark leading, the cloud over them, the prayers to follow, vv.34-36) — Calvin, Clarke, Ellicott, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the ark going before to find a resting place signify?

sides.position: The throne of God's presence leading the way and seeking the rest for his people - a figure of Christ the forerunner who goes before to prepare a place and a rest (Hebrews 6:20; John 14:2-3), the Lord who leads his pilgrim people to the rest that remains (Hebrews 4:9)

themes: And they departed from the mount of the LORD three days' journey: and the ark of the covenant of the LORD went before them in the three days' journey, to search out a resting place for them
the ark of the covenant going before to find a resting place (the crux of Christ going before his people to rest)
the throne of God's presence leading the way and seeking the rest for his people
a figure of Christ the forerunner who goes before to prepare a place and lead to the promised rest (Hebrews 6:20; John 14:2-3; Hebrews 4:9)

schools.summary: The cloud of the LORD over them by day: 'and the cloud of the LORD was upon them by day, when they went out of the camp' — the cloud of the LORD over the people by day as they marched (the the cloud of the LORD was upon them by day, the cloud overshadowing the host, the token of God's presence and protection, the when they went out of the camp, in the march, the figure, the cloud the visible presence of God over his people, the shelter and the guide, the cloud that overshadowed Israel, the presence of God going with them, 'he spread a cloud for a covering,' Psalm 105:39, the figure of God's protecting presence over his pilgrim people, the shadow from the heat and the token of his nearness, the truth that God is with his people in the way, the cloud over them by day, his presence their shelter and guide, 1 Corinthians 10:1); the cloud over the people by day (the presence and shelter, Psalm 105:39) — the cloud (God's presence); 'when they went out of the camp' (the cloud over them in the march, the presence going with them, 1 Corinthians 10:1, the figure, God's protecting presence over the pilgrim people, the providence, the cloud over them, the prayers at the ark to follow, vv.35-36) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the cloud of the LORD was upon them by day, when they went out of the camp
the cloud of the LORD over them by day
the visible presence of God over his people, the shelter and the guide (Psalm 105:39)
God with his pilgrim people in the way (1 Corinthians 10:1)

schools.summary: Moses's prayer at the going forth of the ark: 'and it came to pass, when the ark set forward, that Moses said, Rise up, LORD, and let thine enemies be scattered; and let them that hate thee flee before thee' — the prayer at the march — 'Rise up, LORD, and let thine enemies be scattered; and let them that hate thee flee before thee': whenever the ark set forward, Moses praying that the LORD would arise and scatter his enemies (the witnesses noting the significance — the prayer that God himself, present in the ark, would go before and scatter all who opposed his people, the enemies of Israel reckoned as the enemies of God, the LORD called to arise and put them to flight, the lesson — that the cause of God's people is God's own cause, their enemies his enemies, the appeal to God to arise and act, to scatter those who hate him, the confidence that when God arises none can stand, the wider use — this prayer taken up by David as the opening of the great psalm of God's triumphant march, 'Let God arise, let his enemies be scattered: let them also that hate him flee before him,' Psalm 68:1, and applied to the ascension and triumph of Christ, who, having risen, scatters his enemies and leads captivity captive, Psalm 68:18; Ephesians 4:8, the figure — the prayer for God to arise in the going forth of his people and his gospel, scattering the enemies of his kingdom, the triumphant advance of the presence of God, the confidence of the church on the march, that God arises and his enemies are scattered, the figure of the victorious progress of Christ and his people against all opposition); the prayer offered whenever the ark set forward (the march begun with prayer) — the occasion (the prayer at the going forth); the appeal for God to arise and scatter his enemies (the cause of God's people God's own, Psalm 68:1) — the figure (Christ risen scattering his foes, Ephesians 4:8, the providence, the prayer at the going forth, the prayer at the rest to follow, vv.36) — Calvin, Clarke, Gill.

debates.question: What does Moses's prayer 'Rise up, LORD' signify?

sides.position: The appeal for God himself, present in the ark, to go before and scatter all who oppose his people (their enemies his own) - taken up by David in the great psalm of God's triumph (Psalm 68:1) and applied to the risen Christ who scatters his foes (Psalm 68:18; Ephesians 4:8), the confidence of the church on the march

themes: And it came to pass, when the ark set forward, that Moses said, Rise up, LORD, and let thine enemies be scattered; and let them that hate thee flee before thee
Moses's prayer at the going forth of the ark (the crux of the prayer for God to arise against his enemies)
the prayer for God himself to go before and scatter all who oppose his people, their enemies his own
taken up in the psalm of God's triumph and applied to the risen Christ who scatters his foes (Psalm 68:1, 18; Ephesians 4:8)

schools.summary: Moses's prayer at the resting of the ark: 'and when it rested, he said, Return, O LORD, unto the many thousands of Israel' — whenever the ark rested, Moses praying that the LORD would return and abide with the multitudes of Israel (the when it rested, the ark stopping, the camp at rest, the Return, O LORD, unto the many thousands of Israel, the prayer for God to abide with his people in the rest, to dwell among the multitudes, the figure, the prayer for God's presence in the rest as in the march, that he abide with his people, the two prayers together — God arising to scatter the foe in the march, and returning to dwell with his people in the rest, the figure of God's presence both leading in the way and dwelling in the rest, 'the LORD shall preserve thy going out and thy coming in,' Psalm 121:8, the desire for God to dwell among the many thousands of his people, the presence of God the joy of the rest, the figure of Christ dwelling with his people, his presence the blessing of both the journey and the rest, the prayer that God would not leave but abide with the great congregation, the church the dwelling of God); the prayer at the ark's resting (the camp at rest) — the occasion (the prayer at the rest); 'Return, O LORD, unto the many thousands of Israel' (the prayer for God to abide with the multitudes, God's presence in the rest, Psalm 121:8, the figure, God dwelling with his people, the presence the joy of the rest, the providence, the prayer at the rest, closing the chapter) — Keil and Delitzsch, Calvin, Gill.

themes: And when it rested, he said, Return, O LORD, unto the many thousands of Israel
Moses's prayer at the resting of the ark
the prayer for God to abide with the multitudes of his people, his presence the blessing of the rest
a figure of Christ dwelling with his people, both in the journey and the rest (Psalm 121:8)
